html:
Silicone Coatings for Roofs That Hold Water After a Storm

Silicone is the coating we specify most often on Worcester roofs with a ponding water problem, since it's the one common coating chemistry that stays stable under standing water instead of breaking down. We size it by mil thickness against the membrane's condition, not as a blanket fix for every aging roof.

Why Ponding Water Rules Out Most Other Coatings

A lot of Worcester's older flat roofs, especially mill buildings with settled decks and institutional buildings with drainage that's degraded over decades, hold water in low spots after every storm rather than draining fully within the 48 hours most codes call for. Acrylic coatings can soften and lose adhesion under prolonged standing water, while silicone holds its chemical structure and stays bonded to the substrate even when water sits for extended periods.

That resistance doesn't fix the underlying drainage problem. A silicone coating over a low spot keeps water out of the building, but the roof still ponds, which means we're always honest with owners about whether a coating is solving the leak or just buying time before a drainage correction becomes necessary.

Mil Thickness and What It Actually Buys

Silicone coatings are typically applied at 20 or 30 mils dry film thickness, and the thicker application costs more but extends the recoat cycle and adds real puncture resistance for roofs with foot traffic. We spec 20-mil on lower-traffic roofs with a straightforward restoration scope and move to 30-mil on roofs with heavier equipment traffic or a documented ponding condition where the extra film thickness pays for itself in service life.

Thickness gets measured, not estimated by eye, since undercoating a roof is one of the most common ways a silicone job fails to deliver the warranty term the owner was quoted.

Restoration Economics Against Full Replacement

A silicone coating over an existing membrane in fair condition typically costs a fraction of full tear-off and replacement, which makes it an attractive option for owners trying to extend a roof's life without a capital reroofing project. The tradeoff is that a coating restores the existing membrane rather than replacing it, so any structural or insulation problems underneath stay exactly as they were.

We inspect the existing membrane's condition closely before recommending a coating over a replacement. A membrane with widespread seam failure, trapped moisture, or significant physical damage isn't a good coating candidate no matter how attractive the price difference looks on paper.

What we check before recommending a silicone coating:

  • Existing membrane condition and whether seams are still sound
  • Moisture content in the insulation below the membrane
  • Ponding water locations and how long water actually sits after a storm
  • Mil thickness needed based on traffic and ponding severity
  • Surface preparation and cleaning requirements before coating
  • Recoat cycle and warranty terms tied to the applied thickness

Recoat Cycle and Long-Term Maintenance

A silicone-coated roof isn't a one-time fix. Most coating systems carry a warranty tied to a recoat cycle, typically somewhere in the ten to fifteen year range depending on thickness and exposure, after which the coating needs a maintenance recoat to keep the warranty active. We build that expectation into the proposal rather than letting an owner assume a coating is a permanent replacement for the membrane underneath.

UV Reflectivity and Summer Heat Gain

Silicone coatings are typically bright white and highly reflective, which reduces solar heat gain the same way a reflective TPO membrane does. On institutional and lab buildings running heavy summer cooling loads, that reflectivity is a real secondary benefit beyond the waterproofing itself.

Surface Prep and Why Skipping It Voids the Warranty

Silicone coating adhesion depends heavily on how clean and dry the existing membrane is at application time. Standing water, algae growth in shaded low spots, and years of accumulated dirt all interfere with proper bonding if the roof isn't power-washed and dried before coating. Manufacturers write their warranties around a documented prep process, and a contractor who skips or shortcuts that step is putting the warranty at risk before the first coat even goes down.

We schedule the prep work as its own step with drying time built in, rather than compressing washing and coating into the same visit, since a membrane that looks dry to the eye can still be holding enough moisture to compromise adhesion.

Questions Worcester Owners Ask About Silicone Coatings

Will a silicone coating fix my roof's ponding water problem?

It stops the ponding from causing a leak, but the water will still sit there. Fixing the ponding itself usually requires a drainage or slope correction separate from the coating.

How thick does the coating need to be?

We typically spec 20 or 30 mils depending on traffic and ponding severity, and we measure the applied thickness rather than estimating it visually.

Is a coating cheaper than a full reroof?

Usually, yes, but only if the existing membrane is a sound candidate. A membrane with widespread seam failure or trapped moisture isn't a good fit for coating.

How long does a silicone coating last before it needs recoating?

It depends on the thickness applied and the roof's exposure, but most systems carry a warranty tied to a recoat cycle rather than a one-time application.

Can silicone be applied over any membrane type?

Most single-ply and modified bitumen membranes are viable candidates after proper cleaning and preparation, but we confirm compatibility with the specific existing membrane before quoting.
